display_name: Interface shows up/up but has late collisions or runts — one side is full-duplex, the other half-duplex; link state does not reveal this
context: An Ethernet interface shows up/up and passes some traffic, but performance is poor and interface counters show late collisions, FCS errors, runts, or alignment errors
observation: The interface is up/up with no protocol errors, but 'show interface' reveals incrementing late collisions (on the half-duplex side) or FCS/runt/alignment errors (on the full-duplex side), and throughput is significantly below the link speed
naive_interpretation: There is a bad cable, a failing NIC, or a switch port hardware issue causing physical layer errors — replace the cable or the port
correct_interpretation: Late collisions on an Ethernet interface almost always indicate a duplex mismatch: one end of the link is operating in full-duplex mode and the other in half-duplex. The link comes up and shows up/up because each side independently negotiates (or is forced to) a valid speed and duplex — neither side is aware of what the other chose. In half-duplex, the device uses CSMA/CD and expects collisions to occur within the first 64 bytes (slot time). When the full-duplex side transmits simultaneously (which it does freely because it believes the link is full-duplex), the half-duplex side detects a collision after the slot time — this is a 'late collision,' which is abnormal in properly functioning Ethernet and nearly pathognomonic for duplex mismatch. The full-duplex side sees FCS errors and runts because the half-duplex side truncates frames when it backs off mid-transmission. The fix is to ensure both sides are set to the same duplex — preferably both on auto-negotiation, or both forced to full-duplex.
why: IEEE 802.3 auto-negotiation failure mode: if one side is forced to full-duplex and the other is set to auto, the auto side cannot detect the forced side's duplex (only speed is detectable via link pulses) and defaults to half-duplex — this is the most common cause of duplex mismatch
The interface link state protocol (up/up) does not negotiate or verify duplex agreement — each side operates independently in its configured duplex mode, so a mismatch is invisible to link state

Late collisions are nearly impossible on a properly functioning full-duplex link (collisions don't exist in full-duplex) and rare on a properly functioning half-duplex link (they require cable length exceeding the collision domain) — their presence is a strong indicator of duplex mismatch
At low traffic rates, the mismatch may not produce symptoms because collisions are probabilistic — the problem only manifests under load when both sides transmit frequently enough to collide

confirmation_commands.purpose: Check the negotiated duplex mode and look for late collisions, runts, or CRC errors — late collisions strongly indicate duplex mismatch
Display duplex setting and error counters — compare duplex on both ends of the link

confirmation_commands.command: show cdp neighbors <interface> detail | include Duplex
confirmation_commands.purpose: If CDP is available, check the remote side's reported duplex without logging into it — a duplex mismatch will be visible in CDP data
Verify negotiated duplex and check for late collision counters

traps: Replacing cables or SFPs when the error pattern (late collisions + runts) points to duplex mismatch — physical layer components do not cause late collisions

Setting one side to 'auto' and the other to 'full-duplex' and assuming they will agree — the auto side detects speed but defaults to half-duplex when it cannot see the other side's duplex advertisement, creating the mismatch
Not noticing the problem at low traffic rates — duplex mismatch only causes collisions when both sides transmit simultaneously, so lightly loaded links may appear fine during testing
Checking only the local side's duplex setting — the mismatch requires comparing BOTH ends; each side's configuration looks valid in isolation

On modern 1G/10G/25G+ links, assuming duplex mismatch is impossible — while most high-speed links are full-duplex only, management interfaces and some legacy 100M connections can still have this issue
